\caption{Calibrated self-financing by damage column: stacked revaluation
(negative) and damage-dividend components against the full-financing line.}
\label{fig:pfig7}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Robustness: what one must believe for green deficits to
self-finance}
\label{subsec:robustness}

The climate side of the calibration deserves to be interrogated rather than
defended, and this subsection organizes that interrogation around a simple
principle: parameters that published damage estimates discipline are
\emph{anchored} to those estimates and swept across them; the one parameter
no study pins down is not given a point value at all, but reported as an
axis of the answer. Table~\ref{tab:sources} states where each climate
parameter comes from.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering\small
\caption{Climate-block parameters: values, status, and sources. ``Anchored''
parameters take their range from published estimates; ``swept'' parameters
are reported as axes of the results rather than point choices.}
\label{tab:sources}
\begin{tabular}{llll}
\toprule
parameter & benchmark & status & source / discipline \\
\midrule
$D_0$ (no-abatement damages) & $0.06$ & anchored, 3 columns &
  \begin{tabular}[t]{@{}l@{}}$0.02$ DICE \citep{nordhaus2017};
  $0.06$ Dell--Jones--Olken /\\ Burke--Hsiang--Miguel range; $0.20$
  \citet{bilalkanzig2024}\end{tabular}\\
$\theta_g$ (abatement effectiveness) & $1.2$ & swept ($0$--$3$) &
  no direct estimate; reported as a frontier (below) \\
$\psi$ (damage incidence) & $0$ & swept ($0$--$2$) &
  regressive incidence: \citet{kanzig2023carbon}, \citet{fried2018} \\
$\phi_D$ (damage--risk link) & $0.5$ & swept ($0$--$1$) &
  reduced form of \citet{acharya2023optimal} risk channel \\
$\delta_g$ (green depreciation) & $0.10$ & swept ($0.05$--$0.10$) &
  public-infrastructure depreciation range \\
program scale & $2\%$ of income & swept ($1$--$4\%$) &
  public green-investment shares in net-zero scenarios \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two exercises then close the gap between ``illustrative'' and ``robust,''
both on the calibrated economy ($\beta^{*}$ holding debt at $1.1$ times
income; the $2\%$ program). \emph{First, the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ surface.}
The two parameters the headline number is most sensitive to are the
no-abatement damage level and the abatement effectiveness. Rather than
defend point values, we compute the self-financing share on the whole
$(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane and report the $\nu=1$ \emph{frontier} --- for each
damage level, the abatement effectiveness required for full self-financing
(Figure~\ref{fig:pfig20}a). The paper's answer is thus a correspondence,
not a point estimate: a reader who holds DICE-level damage beliefs and a
reader who accepts the \citet{bilalkanzig2024} estimates can each locate
their own $\nu$ on the same map, and the map records at every node whether
the revaluation component stays negative --- so the disinflation result is
checked across the entire plane rather than asserted at the benchmark.
\emph{Second, one-at-a-time sensitivity under a held fiscal target.} Each
household-side variant (risk aversion $\sigma\in\{1,3\}$; the risk channel
$\phi_D\in\{0,1\}$; the incidence gradient $\psi\in\{1,2\}$) re-calibrates
$\beta$ so that debt remains $1.1$ times income before comparing programs
--- differences in $\nu$ are then pure parameter effects, never artifacts
of a drifting debt ratio --- while variants that leave the no-program
economy untouched (green depreciation $\delta_g$; the program scale at
$1\%$ and $4\%$) hold $\beta^{*}$ fixed (Figure~\ref{fig:pfig20}b).

Three findings survive the exercise. \emph{First, the required
effectiveness falls sharply in the damage level.} The $\nu=1$ frontier reads
$\theta_g^{*}=2.18$ at the medium column, $0.57$ at the
\citet{bilalkanzig2024} high column, and lies \emph{above} the swept range
($>3$) at the conservative DICE column --- so what one must believe for full
self-financing is monotone and transparent: under high damages even a
modestly effective program pays for itself, while under DICE damages no
effectiveness in the plausible range does, and the honest answer there is
partial financing ($\nu=0.04$--$0.39$ over $\theta_g\in[0.5,3]$) carried by
the damage dividend net of the revaluation loss. The benchmark $\theta_g=1.2$
sits comfortably below the medium-column frontier, consistent with the
partial self-financing ($\nu=0.58$) reported above. \emph{Second, the
disinflation is not a calibration artifact:} the revaluation component is
negative at all $65$
nodes of the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane, so productive green programs raise
safe-asset demand and lower the price level regardless of the damage or
effectiveness assumption. \emph{Third, the self-financing share is stable to
the household side but genuinely governed by incidence.} Holding the debt
target fixed, $\nu$ moves little with risk aversion ($0.57$ at $\sigma=3$)
or the damage--risk link ($0.56$--$0.61$ as $\phi_D$ runs $0$ to $1$), rises
mechanically when green capital is longer-lived ($\nu=1.09$ at
$\delta_g=0.05$, which doubles the capital stock per unit of spending) or
the program is smaller ($0.62$ at $1\%$ versus $0.50$ at $4\%$, the
concavity of abatement), and --- the one economically important
non-monotonicity --- the revaluation channel \emph{flips sign} under strong
regressive incidence: at $\psi=2$ the program becomes mildly inflationary
($\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}=+0.07$, $\Delta\ln P=+5.9\%$), exactly the
incidence-driven reversal that Propositions~\ref{prop:sunspots} and
\ref{prop:financingdesign} identify. The disinflation result is thus robust
across damage calibrations and effectiveness but is, by design, a knife-edge
in the incidence gradient --- which is the paper's point, not a fragility.
Two variants are informative by their absence: $\sigma=1$ is omitted because
the incomplete-markets economy cannot generate debt of $1.1$ times income at
the calibrated real rate when risk aversion is that low (the precautionary
motive is too weak --- a restriction on the model, not a numerical failure),
which is itself why the benchmark uses $\sigma=2$.

% Figure PFig20 (calibration robustness) relocated to
% Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Why the price level moves: the safe-asset channel isolated}
\label{subsec:safeasset}

The green disinflation result invites an obvious question: \emph{which}
part of the program moves the price level --- the taxes that pay for it,
the damages it avoids, the income risk it removes, or the instrument that
finances it? The steady-state equilibrium condition $P^{*} = B/S(1+r^{ss})$
makes this answerable by exact counterfactuals: we re-solve the economy
switching on one margin at a time and decompose
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0$ into a tax-burden contribution (program cost, no
climate benefits), a damage level-and-incidence contribution (damages at
the program level, risk held fixed), a risk contribution (income-risk
level at the program value, endowments held fixed --- the $\phi_D$
channel), and an interaction term reported explicitly rather than absorbed.
Each counterfactual is a well-posed economy whose own budget identity
holds, so no node relies on off-equilibrium objects; the financing
contribution is the levy-versus-lump-sum swap at the same program. All
figures in this subsection are steady-state comparisons on the
full-resolution grid ($n_a = 500$) at the calibrated medium-damage scale.

This decomposition splits the total green disinflation of
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0 = -5.23\%$ (from $P_0 = 0.9051$ to $P_1 = 0.8590$)
into:
\[
\underbrace{-5.77\%}_{\text{tax burden}}
\;\underbrace{-\,1.57\%}_{\text{damage level/incid.}}
\;\underbrace{+\,2.08\%}_{\text{risk}}
\;\underbrace{+\,0.03\%}_{\text{interaction}},
\]
with partial-equilibrium asset demand moving from $S(\tau_0, D_0) = 1.105$
to $S(\tau_1, D_0) = 1.169$ (taxes), $S(\tau_0, D_1^{\mathrm{lev}}) =
1.122$ (damage level), and $S(\tau_0, D_1^{\mathrm{risk}}) = 1.084$
(risk); the near-zero interaction term says the three channels are
additive to two decimal places at this scale. Two lessons, one expected
and one not. The expected one: the tax
burden dominates --- lump-sum taxes push constraint-adjacent households
toward precautionary saving, asset demand rises, and the price level
falls, which is the core of green disinflation. The unexpected one: the
\emph{two climate channels have opposite signs}. Avoided damages are
themselves disinflationary ($-1.57\%$): richer households save more in
levels, so the damage dividend \emph{amplifies} the bondholder windfall
rather than offsetting it. Only the risk channel works toward inflation
($+2.08\%$): with less climate-driven income risk, precautionary demand
falls. Green disinflation is therefore not a tax artifact --- it survives
even at zero program cost through the damage-level node --- and its only
countervailing force is the insurance content of climate policy. The
financing swap confirms the regime section at the level of asset demand:
moving the same program from lump-sum to levy financing moves the price
level by $+8.01$ log points, larger than the entire program effect
itself.

\pfigguard{PFig15_safe_asset_decomposition.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig15_safe_asset_decomposition.pdf}
\caption{The safe-asset channel isolated: (a) exact log contributions of
the tax-burden, damage, risk, and interaction margins to
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0$ (GE counterfactuals); (b) partial-equilibrium asset
demand at the fixed-$(\tau, D)$ nodes.}
\label{fig:pfig15}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Aggregate climate risk: the safe asset made literal}
\label{subsec:aggrisk}

Everything to this point has been deterministic, so the ``safe asset''
of the mechanism is safe in real terms too, and the phrase is a
metaphor. This subsection removes the metaphor by adding aggregate climate
risk. Let the aggregate climate state follow a two-state
Markov chain --- \textsc{calm} ($D^0=0.06$) and \textsc{severe}
($D^0=0.20$, the high-damage column of Section~\ref{sec:quant}) --- with
a small annual probability of entering the severe state and a mean severe
spell of a few years ($\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}=[0.95,0.05;\,0.30,0.70]$).
Households still hold only the one-period nominal bond; markets are
incomplete in the aggregate as well as the idiosyncratic dimension. The
price level is now measurable in the aggregate state, $(P_C,P_S)$, and
the bond's \emph{real} return is state-contingent,
\[
1 + r(s\to s') = (1+r^{ss})\,\frac{P_s}{P_{s'}},
\qquad 1+r^{ss}=\frac{1+i^{ss}}{1+\mu},
\]
where $r^{ss}$ is the deterministic real service rate of
\eqref{eq:fisher}. So a nominally safe bond is really risky: it loses real
value in exactly
the state where households are poorest. The DTPL becomes a vector fixed
point --- the bond market clears in \emph{each} aggregate state,
$S_s(P_C,P_S)=B/P_s$ --- solved on the exact ergodic joint distribution
over $(a,e,s)$ (household problem in Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}).

The equilibrium prices this climate risk sharply. At the calibrated
benchmark the price level is $37\%$ higher in the severe state than in the
calm state ($P_S/P_C-1 = 0.37$), so a household that holds the nominal
bond into a climate disaster earns $+2.0\%$ in the calm state but takes a
\textbf{real loss of $26\%$} on the transition $\textsc{calm}\to\textsc{severe}$
--- the nominal safe asset is a poor hedge against systematic climate
risk, and carries a corresponding risk premium (the bond's expected real
return exceeds the deterministic real rate by $0.4$ percentage points).
This is the aggregate counterpart of the paper's central object: the same
incomplete-markets demand for the nominal liability that pins the price
level also determines how badly that liability performs when the climate
turns.

\emph{Green public investment lowers this systematic risk.} A permanent
program of two percent of income builds abatement capital that reduces
damages in both states through the same climate block used everywhere
($D_s = D^0_s e^{-\theta_g K_g}$), and because the severe state starts
from a higher damage level the program compresses the gap between the two:
the state price dispersion falls from $0.37$ to $0.27$ and the disaster
real loss shrinks from $26\%$ to $20\%$ (the bond risk premium falls from
$0.44$ to $0.25$ percentage points). Green investment is therefore not
only a level intervention on average damages (the damage dividend of
Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}) but a \emph{reduction in the aggregate
climate risk that the safe nominal asset must bear} --- a channel that
does not exist in the deterministic model and that makes the
safe-asset language of the paper literal (Figure~\ref{fig:pfig19}).%
\footnote{These magnitudes are computed on the same full-resolution asset
grid as the deterministic benchmark ($n_a=500$); both the calm and severe
fixed points converge. Adding the aggregate-risk term to the self-financing
decomposition and to the welfare analysis is a natural extension.}

\pfigguard{PFig19_aggrisk.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig19_aggrisk.pdf}
\caption{Aggregate climate risk ($n_a=500$). Two recurrent
aggregate states (\textsc{calm}/\textsc{severe}); the nominal bond is
nominally safe but really risky ($B/P_s$ state-contingent). (a) the two
state-contingent
price levels, baseline vs.\ a $2\%$-of-income green program; (b) the state
price dispersion $P_S/P_C-1$; (c) the bond's real return on a
$\textsc{calm}\to\textsc{severe}$ transition (the disaster return). Green
investment compresses the dispersion ($0.37\to0.27$) and shrinks the
disaster loss ($-26\%\to-20\%$) --- it reduces the systematic climate risk
the safe asset carries.}
\label{fig:pfig19}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{Where the risk compression shows up: welfare, not the budget.}
It is tempting to add a fourth term to the self-financing decomposition ---
the fiscal value of the compressed premium. That term is exactly zero, for
a clean reason. The government's ergodic-expected real interest bill is
\begin{equation}
\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'} \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\,\big(R(s,s')-1\big)\,\frac{B}{P_s}
\;=\; r^{ss}\,B\,\E_\pi\!\left[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\right],
\label{eq:premiuminert}
\end{equation}
which is \emph{independent of the risk premium}: with
$R(s,s')=(1+r^{ss})P_s/P_{s'}$ and ergodic $\pi$, the state-contingent
returns average to $r^{ss}$ times the debt, and the premium --- a
risk-\emph{timing} wedge that makes the bond expensive to service in the
severe state and cheap in the calm state --- washes out in expectation
(Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}). The aggregate-risk self-financing share is
therefore $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$, the ergodic average
of the deterministic decomposition, with no separate aggregate-risk term;
the driver confirms \eqref{eq:premiuminert} numerically to machine precision
(the direct ergodic bill and the closed form agree to $3\times10^{-18}$). In
the calibrated aggregate-risk economy the self-financing share is
$\nu=0.77$ ($\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}=0.85$, $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}=-0.08$),
\emph{above} the medium-column deterministic value of $0.56$ --- not because
aggregate risk helps the budget, but because the ergodic-mean damage is
higher when a high-damage severe state is possible, so the expected damage
dividend is larger.

The premium is instead a \emph{risk-sharing} object, and its compression is
priced in welfare. We isolate it with an equal-mean-reduction
counterfactual: the actual program lowers damages more in the severe state
(compressing the price dispersion), while the counterfactual lowers both
states by the same amount, so the ergodic-mean damage falls identically but
the dispersion does not compress. The consumption-equivalent gap between the
two is the pure value of \emph{preferentially} insuring the severe state ---
$+0.13\%$ of permanent consumption on average, which offsets about a tenth of
the program's steady-state welfare cost ($-1.35\%$ at the calibrated medium
scale, before transition gains). The insurance value falls exactly where the
mechanism says it should: it is about six times larger for a household
\emph{in} the severe state ($+0.46\%$) than in the calm state ($+0.08\%$) ---
the compression is valued when the disaster bites. Across the wealth
distribution it is essentially flat ($+0.14\%$ for the bottom quintile and
the constrained, $+0.14\%$ for the top), because at the benchmark's
\emph{uniform} damage incidence ($\psi=0$) the compression is a proportional
income gain valued alike up and down the distribution; under the regressive
incidence of Propositions~\ref{prop:sunspots} and
\ref{prop:financingdesign} ($\psi>0$), where damages fall on the poor, the
same exercise tilts the insurance value toward the constrained households who
hold the nominally-safe, really-risky bond as their only buffer.

% Figure PFig21 (aggregate-risk Stage B welfare) relocated to
% Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Welfare incidence by wealth group}
\label{subsec:welfaregroups}

Who pays for the program and who benefits? For each state $(a,e)$ we compute
the permanent consumption transfer $\lambda(a,e)$ that makes a household at
that state indifferent between the no-program and program steady states,
aggregated over baseline-distribution wealth quintiles
(Table~\ref{tab:incidence}; generated by \texttt{main\_project\_calibrated}
section C4).

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Consumption-equivalent welfare incidence of the program under
deficit (lump-sum) financing, by baseline wealth quintile (percent;
calibrated pass, $n_a=500$ run).}
\label{tab:incidence}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
 & Q1 (poorest) & Q2 & Q3 & Q4 & Q5 & top 10\% & aggregate \\
\midrule
LOW (DICE)          & $-3.57$ & $-2.85$ & $-2.57$ & $-2.28$ & $-1.89$ & $-1.75$ & $-2.64$ \\
MEDIUM (DJO--BHM)   & $-2.60$ & $-1.90$ & $-1.66$ & $-1.42$ & $-1.12$ & $-1.02$ & $-1.75$ \\
HIGH (Bilal--K\"anzig) & $+1.82$ & $+2.33$ & $+2.40$ & $+2.40$ & $+2.29$ & $+2.24$ & $+2.25$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two findings. First, \textbf{deficit-financed green investment is regressive
at the steady-state margin}: in the low and medium columns the poorest
quintile loses the most ($-3.57\%$ versus $-1.89\%$ for the richest in the
low column), because the lump-sum tax that services the program bites
hardest for households at the borrowing constraint, while the price-level
decline hands the windfall to bondholders at the top. Even in the
high-damage column --- where every quintile gains and the program is a
steady-state Pareto improvement across wealth groups --- the poorest gain
\emph{least} ($+1.82\%$ versus $+2.40\%$ at Q3--Q4), for the same reason. The
distributional politics of green deficits under conventional financing are
therefore inverted relative to the inflation-tax intuition: it is the
\emph{poor} who carry the program and the bondholders who are compensated.
Second, this regressivity is a property of the \emph{financing}, not of the
program --- which motivates the regime comparison below, where the same real
program is financed progressively and the ranking reverses.

\pfigguard{PFig8_welfare_incidence.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{PFig8_welfare_incidence.pdf}
\caption{Welfare incidence by baseline wealth quintile across damage
columns (deficit financing).}
\label{fig:pfig8}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{Extended groups.} The package additionally reports incidence by
income-state quintile, by bondholding quintile (in the one-asset economy
the only asset \emph{is} the government bond, so wealth and bondholding
quintiles coincide --- a fact worth stating rather than hiding), for
constrained versus unconstrained households, and for a high-MPC proxy
group (the top quartile of the consumption-policy slope $dC/dA$ on the
baseline policy; a proxy, labeled as such). These are steady-state
comparisons at the calibrated medium-damage scale. Under
\textsc{r1-deficit}, every cut of
the population points the same way: income-state quintiles lose
monotonically ($-2.73\%$, $-2.01\%$, $-1.62\%$, $-1.34\%$, $-1.05\%$ from
bottom to top; exact fractional apportioning of the seven-state process
across quintile boundaries), constrained households ($17.7\%$ of the
population) lose $-2.64\%$ against $-1.56\%$ for the unconstrained, and
the high-MPC quartile loses $-2.25\%$ against $-1.56\%$ for the rest ---
the program under deficit finance is paid for precisely by the households
with the least self-insurance capacity. Under
\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate} every one of those orderings \emph{reverses}:
the income quintiles gain $+1.86\%$, $+1.02\%$, $+0.53\%$, $+0.16\%$,
$-0.24\%$ from bottom to top, the constrained gain $+1.71\%$ against
$+0.44\%$, and the high-MPC group $+1.23\%$ against $+0.45\%$, at an
aggregate gain of $+0.67\%$ against the deficit regime's $-1.75\%$.
Stated conditionally: incidence is regressive under pure deficit finance
in the low and medium damage calibrations, rebate financing reverses
bottom-half losses, and in the high-damage column all groups gain --- but
full self-financing remains conditional on the high-damage,
high-effectiveness region.

% Figure PFig16 (extended welfare groups) relocated to
% Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Financing regimes: deficit, proportional levy, rebate, mixed}
\label{subsec:regimes}

Green spending is not carbon policy, and financing design is a choice. The
package compares four ways of financing the \emph{same} real program at the
calibrated medium-damage column: (\textsc{r1-deficit}) lump-sum/deficit
financing (the baseline); (\textsc{r2-prop-levy}) a proportional levy on
effective endowments that funds the program; (\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate})
the levy at twice the program with half rebated lump-sum (the progressive
rebate design suggested by the incidence evidence in
\citealp{kanzig2023carbon}); and (\textsc{r4-mixed-deficit-levy}) an even
deficit/levy mix. All four satisfy the same aggregate budget identity
$\tau_{ls} + \vartheta\,(1-D) = r^{ss} b + g_g$, so aggregate resources are
identical: the regimes differ only in \emph{incidence} --- proportional
versus lump-sum --- transmitted through precautionary asset demand to the
price level, the self-financing share, and group welfare. In the endowment
HA-DTPL block, the levy should be interpreted as a \emph{proportional
financing instrument with carbon-tax-style incidence}. The Pigouvian
carbon-tax margin requires the clean/dirty production extension and is not
claimed in this section: neither instrument distorts a margin here and the
levy carries no abatement incentive (abatement is public). What this
comparison isolates is the pure incidence-and-price-level content of
financing design --- which, given that the revaluation channel is an
incidence object, is exactly where the DTPL mechanism has bite.
Table~\ref{tab:regimes} reports the run (medium damages, calibrated scale;
no-program baseline $P_0 = 0.9051$).

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Financing regimes for the same real green program
(medium damages, calibrated scale, $n_a=500$ run). $\lambda_{b50}$,
$\lambda_{t10}$: consumption-equivalent gains of the bottom 50\% and top
10\% of the baseline wealth distribution.}
\label{tab:regimes}
\begin{tabular}{lcccccc}
\toprule
Regime & $P^{*}$ & $\nu$ & $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ & $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ &
$\lambda_{b50}$ & $\lambda_{t10}$ \\
\midrule
\textsc{r1-deficit} (lump-sum)      & 0.859 & 0.581 & $-0.055$ & 0.636 & $-2.16\%$ & $-1.02\%$ \\
\textsc{r2-prop-levy}               & 0.931 & 0.672 & $+0.030$ & 0.642 & $-0.36\%$ & $-0.52\%$ \\
\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate}        & 0.999 & \textbf{0.758} & $\mathbf{+0.111}$ & 0.647 & $\mathbf{+1.13\%}$ & $-0.12\%$ \\
\textsc{r4-mixed-deficit-levy}      & 0.896 & 0.628 & $-0.011$ & 0.639 & $-1.21\%$ & $-0.76\%$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Three results, the first of which we regard as the sharpest in the paper.
First, \textbf{the sign of the revaluation channel is a policy choice}
(Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}): moving from lump-sum to
proportional financing flips green disinflation ($\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} =
-0.055$) into mild green inflation ($+0.030$; $+0.111$ with the rebate).
The mechanism is the asset-demand incidence at the heart of the DTPL:
lump-sum taxes push constraint-adjacent households toward their borrowing
limit and \emph{raise} precautionary demand (the price level falls, the
fisc loses the revaluation); a proportional levy scales endowments and
\emph{lowers} demand roughly proportionally (the price level rises, real
debt erodes). Second, the progressive design wins on \emph{both} margins
simultaneously: the levy-plus-rebate regime is the most self-financing
($\nu = 0.758$, a fifth more than deficit financing) \emph{and} the most
progressive (the bottom half gains $+1.13\%$ against $-2.16\%$ under
deficit financing, while the top decile is nearly unharmed at $-0.12\%$
versus $-1.02\%$) --- a near-Pareto dominance over the conventional deficit
regime. Third, the magnitudes quantify how much the DTPL incidence channel
is worth: all four regimes purchase the identical real program with
identical aggregate resources, yet the bottom half's welfare differs by
$3.3$ percentage points of permanent consumption between the worst and best
design. Financing structure is not fiscal bookkeeping in this economy; it
is a price-level and distribution instrument.

\pfigguard{PFig9_financing_regimes.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig9_financing_regimes.pdf}
\caption{Financing regimes: (a) self-financing decomposition; (b) welfare
incidence, bottom 50\% versus top 10\%.}
\label{fig:pfig9}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Bounding the revaluation channel: maturity, indexation, holders}
\label{subsec:maturity}

The inflating-away literature disciplines how much fiscal room nominal-debt
revaluation can really provide: \citet{hilscher2022} show that maturity,
holder composition and the persistence of inflation sharply limit it;
\citet{hallsargent2011} supply the maturity-adjusted accounting;
\citet{doepkeschneider2006} and \citet{auclert2019} the redistribution
mapping. Our framework adds a clarifying twist. In steady-state comparisons
at unchanged $(i,\mu)$, the program moves the price level once --- a
\emph{level jump} --- and a level jump revalues all nominal promises
proportionally \emph{regardless of maturity}: duration neither shields nor
amplifies it. The binding limits are instead (i) \emph{indexation leakage}
--- a share $\alpha_I$ of indexed debt does not revalue, so the fiscal
component scales by $(1-\alpha_I)$; and (ii) \emph{holder composition} ---
a share $\alpha_F$ of foreign-held nominal debt leaves the fiscal gain
unchanged but reallocates the domestic incidence by
$(1-\alpha_I)(1-\alpha_F)$. Because our calibrated revaluation channel is
\emph{negative} (green disinflation), these leakages work in the
government's favor: indexed debt shrinks the fiscal loss, and foreign
holders absorb part of the bondholder windfall that would otherwise accrue
to wealthy domestic households. Duration proper enters only where the real
rate moves --- the accommodation experiments --- through the repricing of
legacy long bonds (geometric-coupon price $q = 1/(1+r-\delta_m)$). The
replication package reports the maturity-adjusted
share $\nu^{M} = (1-\alpha_I)\,\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} + \nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$
over $\alpha_I \in \{0, 0.10, 0.25\}$, the domestic-incidence table over
$\alpha_F \in \{0, 0.30, 0.50\}$, and the duration term for the
$\mu = 0.02 \to 0.03$ accommodation move (the extended-run stance, real
rate $1.96\% \to 0.97\%$) at coupon-decay rates $\delta_m \in \{0, 0.8,
0.9\}$ --- durations of roughly one, five and eight years; the
implementation-efficiency sweep $q_g \in \{0.6, 0.8, 1.0\}$
(planning delays and procurement frictions in the spirit of
\citealp{leeperwalkeryang2010}, disciplined by \citealp{bomligthart2014}
and the IMF's climate public-investment framework) scales the damage
dividend correspondingly.

The run (calibrated medium column) delivers three quantitative statements.
First, because the revaluation channel is negative, \emph{indexation raises
the self-financing share}: $\nu^{M} = 0.583$, $0.588$ and $0.596$ at indexed
shares $\alpha_I = 0$, $0.10$ and $0.25$ --- indexed debt shields the fisc
from the disinflation loss, the mirror image of the usual
inflating-away logic. Second, foreign holders absorb the windfall: by the
incidence identity $(1-\alpha_I)(1-\alpha_F)\,\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$, the
domestic revaluation incidence shrinks from $-0.048$ to $-0.024$ as the
foreign-held share rises from zero to one half (at $\alpha_I = 0.10$).
Third, duration amplifies who gains from \emph{accommodation}: under the
$\mu = 0.02 \to 0.03$ move the legacy bond repricing hands holders
$+6.2\%$ at one-year duration, $+10.2\%$ at five years and $+14.7\%$ at
eight years --- accommodation redistributes toward long-bond
holders, in line with the \citet{hallsargent2011} accounting.
Implementation frictions scale the damage dividend nearly linearly:
$\nu = 0.583$, $0.466$ and $0.344$ at $q_g = 1.0$, $0.8$ and $0.6$
(the revaluation component barely moves), so a forty
percent efficiency loss forfeits roughly forty percent of self-financing
--- quantifying why climate public-investment management
(Climate-PIMA-style) is a fiscal issue, not only an engineering one.

% Figures PFig10 (maturity bounds) and PFig11 (implementation efficiency)
% relocated to Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Transition diagnostics}\label{sec:transitions}
% ==========================================================================

All quantitative results so far are \emph{steady-state} comparisons. This
section reports three complementary dynamic exercises, each described by
what it is and --- as importantly --- by what it is not. The first two are
\emph{Phillips-curve} exercises: a representative-agent New Keynesian
transition (Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans}) and a set of linearized
heterogeneous-agent impulse responses (Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1}).
Neither implements the demand-theory price-level mechanism --- in both,
inflation is determined by a New Keynesian Phillips curve and an
interest-rate rule, not by asset-market clearing of the nominal debt stock.
We report them because they answer two questions the steady-state analysis
cannot: does the announced program destabilize the nominal block on the way
to the new steady state, and how does the answer depend on the monetary
regime? Both exercises embed the same household and climate blocks as the
model of Section~\ref{sec:model} in a New Keynesian production economy;
that production economy is stated in full in
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}, and the household block of the heterogeneous-agent
version is the incomplete-markets problem of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} augmented with an intratemporal labor
choice. The third exercise (Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}) is the paper's own
mechanism in dynamics --- the nonlinear heterogeneous-agent transition in
which the price-level \emph{path} is the unknown pinned by asset demand,
with no Phillips curve anywhere --- and its converged solution turns the
steady-state revaluation result into an announcement effect: the green
disinflation is front-loaded into the announcement year, and its inflation
response has the \emph{opposite sign} to the two Phillips-curve exercises',
which is precisely the observable content of the mechanism.

\subsection{Representative-agent New Keynesian transitions}
\label{subsec:ranktrans}

A representative-agent New Keynesian economy with Rotemberg pricing (stated
in full in Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}) carries
the same climate block as the quantitative model (green public capital
$K_g$, carbon stock $X$, TFP damages $D$; identical parameters). A
permanent green-investment program of the calibrated steady-state scale
phases in linearly over twelve quarters (implementation delays in the
spirit of \citet{leeperwalkeryang2010}) and is financed \emph{contemporaneously by
taxes} under a debt-stabilizing fiscal rule --- by design, so that the
exercise isolates the real/nominal transition shape from the deficit
question studied everywhere else in the paper. Perfect-foresight paths
(300 quarters) are computed under four regimes: a weakly active rule
(\textsc{weak}: $\phi_\pi=1.1$, $\rho_i=0.5$, the closest numerically
regular stand-in for a peg), an inertial Taylor rule (\textsc{taylor}:
$\phi_\pi=1.5$, $\rho_i=0.8$), strict inflation targeting
(\textsc{aggressive}: $\phi_\pi=3$), and a temporary green accommodation
(\textsc{greenaccom}: Taylor plus a rate cut proportional to the
green-capital gap, $\psi_g=0.03$, fading as $K_g$ accumulates). The
accommodation is deliberately large: the un-smoothed stance target implies
a cut of about seven annualized percentage points at the program's start,
though the rule's own inertia ($\rho_i=0.8$) means the realized first-period
cut is nearer one and a half points, building thereafter.

Three findings, all verified by the converged runs (solver residuals
$\sim 10^{-14}$; the $K_g$ accumulation identity holds to $10^{-9}$ on
every reported path):

\begin{enumerate}
\item \emph{The tax-financed program is essentially price-stable under any
active rule.} Impact inflation is $-0.04$\% (annualized) under
\textsc{weak}, $-0.52$\% under \textsc{taylor}, $-0.24$\% under
\textsc{aggressive}; peak inflation along the entire transition stays
below $+1.6$\% annualized in all three. A ramped green program of
realistic scale is not, by itself, an inflationary event in the NK block.
\item \emph{The real green transition is monetary-regime independent.}
Green capital reaches $0.349$ (58\% of its terminal level) after 40
quarters and damages fall from $0.0972$ to $0.0925$ in all three active
regimes --- identical to three decimals. The monetary regime redistributes
the nominal path, not the green capital path.
\item \emph{Accommodation buys inflation, not green capital.} The large
\textsc{greenaccom} experiment produces a $+22.6$\% annualized
impact-inflation spike, yet after 40 quarters green capital is the same
($0.349$) and damages are slightly \emph{higher} ($0.0933$): the
demand boom raises output and hence emissions along the way. In the NK
block, tying rate cuts to the green-capital gap is a pure nominal
intervention --- a transition-dynamics counterpart of the steady-state
accommodation-neutrality result of Section~\ref{sec:quant}, and further
motivation for the anchor-insulation design of
Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}.
\end{enumerate}

\pfigguard{PFig13_rank_transitions.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig13_rank_transitions.pdf}
\caption{Representative-agent New Keynesian transitions: perfect-foresight
paths for a permanent tax-financed green program (12-quarter ramp-in) under
four monetary regimes --- a weakly active rule ($\phi_\pi=1.1$), an inertial
Taylor rule ($\phi_\pi=1.5$), strict inflation targeting ($\phi_\pi=3.0$),
and a temporary green accommodation whose large inflation spike is the
point of the experiment (accommodation buys inflation, not green capital).
These paths do not implement the demand-theory
price-level mechanism; inflation is set by the Phillips curve and the
interest-rate rule.}
\label{fig:pfig13}
\end{figure}}

\subsection{Linearized heterogeneous-agent impulse responses}
\label{subsec:hanktier1}

The second exercise restores household heterogeneity: a one-asset
heterogeneous-agent New Keynesian economy (borrowing constraint,
idiosyncratic efficiency risk) solved with sequence-space Jacobians,
carrying the same climate block, a \emph{nominal} policy rate together
with the ex-post Fisher equation --- so that surprise inflation revalues
household asset positions state by state, the linearized counterpart of
the paper's redistribution channel --- and a slow debt-stabilizing tax
rule, so that a quasi-permanent green-investment expansion (persistence
$0.98$, one percent of steady-state output) is \emph{deficit-financed on
impact}. Its household block is the incomplete-markets problem of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} with an intratemporal labor choice added,
and the New Keynesian production and policy blocks are shared with the
representative-agent economy; the complete system is in
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}. Discount factor and labor disutility are
calibrated to clear the asset and labor markets at a damaged steady state
with debt at the calibration target of Section~\ref{sec:quant}; the income
process is a three-state benchmark rather than the seven-state process of
the quantitative model, so magnitudes here are indicative, not calibrated
results.

Its role is deliberately limited: it bridges the representative-agent
diagnostics and the full nonlinear transition, showing that the qualitative
transition shapes survive heterogeneity and Fisher redistribution. It does
not --- and in a linearized New Keynesian setting cannot --- deliver
price-\emph{level} determination by asset demand.
Figure~\ref{fig:pfig14} reports the responses of output, inflation, real
debt, green capital, damages, and the ex-post real return under four
monetary regimes analogous to the representative-agent block's, though not
identical to them: the heterogeneous-agent rules omit interest-rate inertia
and the accommodation keys off the program flow rather than the
green-capital gap, so the \textsc{greenaccom} experiment here is far milder
than its representative-agent namesake and the two are compared on shapes,
not on levels.

The verified run (five regimes solved; calibrated $\beta = 0.9796$;
one-percent-of-output program; persistence $\rho_g = 0.98$) delivers the
exercise's key message in two comparisons. \emph{Across monetary rules},
impact inflation is $+2.53\%$ annualized under \textsc{weak}, $+0.44\%$
under \textsc{taylor}, $+0.10\%$ under \textsc{aggressive}, $+0.65\%$
under \textsc{greenaccom}, with output up half a percent of its
steady-state level on impact ($+0.0044$ in levels against $\bar Y =
0.90$) --- positive throughout, against impact deflation in the
tax-financed, ramped RANK experiment of Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans}
--- and the real green path is again rule-independent (green capital
$+0.148$, damages $-0.0032$ after 40 quarters, identical to three
decimals across all regimes). \emph{Within the Taylor rule}, a fifth
regime (\textsc{taylorbal}) re-runs the identical program with a fast
debt-stabilizing tax rule in place of the slow one, isolating the
deficit-financing component while holding the model fixed: real debt
after 40 quarters rises $+0.007$ instead of $+0.071$ --- the debt buildup
is almost entirely a financing choice --- yet impact inflation barely
moves ($+0.36\%$ against $+0.44\%$). The
decomposition is sharp: in the linearized HANK, \emph{deficit financing
buys the debt path, not the inflation path} --- the mild impact inflation
belongs to the unanticipated program itself, its size is set by the
monetary rule, and the financing margin prices into the fisc's balance
sheet rather than into goods prices. (The steady-state DTPL analysis is
where the financing margin does move the price level --- through
asset-demand incidence, Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} --- which is
precisely the mechanism a linearized NK Phillips curve cannot represent;
the contrast between the two exercises is thus itself informative.) These
magnitudes are indicative, not calibrated results: the income process is a
three-state benchmark and the responses are linearized.

\pfigguard{PFig14_hank_green_irfs.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig14_hank_green_irfs.pdf}
\caption{Linearized heterogeneous-agent impulse responses to a
quasi-permanent deficit-financed green-investment shock (one percent of
steady-state output, persistence $0.98$) under five monetary/financing
regimes: three inflation-coefficient variants ($\phi_\pi = 1.1$, $1.5$,
$3.0$), a temporary green accommodation, and the Taylor rule under
near-balanced-budget financing (\textsc{taylorbal}), whose gap to
\textsc{taylor} isolates the deficit-financing component.
The ex-post real return panel is the linearized Fisher revaluation
channel. These are linearized responses, not the nonlinear
demand-theory price-level transition.}
\label{fig:pfig14}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{A two-asset extension.} The replication package also implements a
two-asset version of this exercise --- liquid nominal bonds versus illiquid
equity with convex portfolio-adjustment costs, sticky wages and prices,
Tobin's-$Q$ investment, and endogenous government debt --- so that the
demand for the liquid nominal asset, the $B/P$ margin at the heart of the
model, is observable separately from total wealth. It carries one
methodological lesson worth stating in the text: making the liquidity
premium an \emph{endogenous convenience yield} that clears the liquid-bond
market is boundary-singular in a truncated sequence-space system under both
natural timings of the premium (the contemporaneous premium acts only
through an income effect on predetermined holdings at the impact date; the
issuance-timed premium leaves the terminal-date premium outside every
within-horizon equation). The extension therefore closes with a single
total-wealth clearing condition and a constant premium, and the endogenous
convenience yield is left to future work. Because a two-asset sequence-space
solve is numerically delicate, the responses are reported only after an
explicit accuracy protocol clears: an oscillation diagnostic on the
differenced responses (all four regimes score one, the minimum) and a
refinement re-solve on a finer illiquid grid that must agree with the
baseline before any magnitude is reported (maximum relative deviation
$6.7\%$ over the first $120$ quarters, inside the $10\%$ gate). The grids
remain coarse ($n_e=3$, $n_b=15$, $n_a=30$), so the magnitudes are
indicative rather than calibrated.

The results (Table~\ref{tab:twoasset}, Figure~\ref{fig:pfig17}) confirm the
one-asset findings under the richer portfolio and add the two objects the
extension was built to see. Three findings carry over. \emph{The real green
path is again rule-independent}: green capital after forty quarters is
$+0.165$ and avoided damages $-0.0039$ in \emph{every} regime, identical to
three decimals --- the monetary rule moves nominal quantities, not the
accumulation of abatement capital. \emph{Deficit financing buys the debt
path, not the inflation path}: the balanced-budget comparator
(\textsc{taylorbal}) accumulates real debt of only $+0.008$ after forty
quarters against $+0.074$--$+0.077$ under the slow debt-stabilizing rule ---
a tenfold gap --- while impact inflation is essentially unchanged
($+0.03\%$ against $+0.04\%$ annualized). And \emph{the richer supply side
dampens impact inflation}: under the Taylor rule it is $+0.04\%$ annualized
(against $+0.44\%$ in the one-asset exercise), reinforcing the message that
the inflationary margin is the \emph{financing}, not the program. The two
new objects are the point of the extension. The \emph{liquid-bond return}
$r^b$ falls on impact and mirrors inflation almost exactly (e.g.\ $-0.0017$
against $\pi=+0.0017$ under \textsc{weak}): the Fisher revaluation lands
specifically on households' \emph{liquid} nominal savings --- the $B/P$
margin the one-asset exercise cannot separate --- rather than on total
wealth. And the \emph{equity price} drops $5$--$7\%$ on impact as the
illiquid asset reprices against the program's future debt and tax path,
most (a $7.2\%$ drop) under the balanced-budget rule, where taxes rise
fastest. The extension thus makes the liquid-safe-asset margin at the heart
of the paper directly observable, and it does so under a substantially
richer household portfolio and production block; its economics are otherwise
covered by the one-asset responses (Fisher redistribution) and by the
nonlinear transition below (the $B/P$ margin proper).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The nonlinear price-level transition}\label{subsec:tier2}

The two exercises above borrow their inflation dynamics from a Phillips
curve. The paper's own mechanism makes a starker prediction about the
transition, and we compute it directly. At $t=1$ the government unexpectedly announces
the permanent program; thereafter foresight is perfect. The unknown is
the \emph{price-level path} $\{P_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$: at every date the price
level must clear the asset market,
\[
S_t\bigl(\{r_s, \tau_s, D_s\}_{s \ge t};\, V_T,\, \Omega_t\bigr)
\;=\; \frac{B_t}{P_t}, \qquad
1 + r_t = (1+i^{ss})\,\frac{P_{t-1}}{P_t},
\]
where $S_t$ is exact aggregate asset demand from the household problem
solved \emph{backward} from the green terminal steady state under the
time-varying taxes, damages, and realized real returns implied by the
trial price path, and the wealth distribution $\Omega_t$ rolls
\emph{forward} from the pre-announcement stationary distribution. There
is no Phillips curve and no policy rule for inflation anywhere in this
computation: the inflation path \emph{is} the repricing of the nominal
debt stock against precautionary asset demand, period by period, and the
$t=1$ jump of $P_1$ against the pre-announcement price level is the
surprise revaluation of outstanding nominal positions --- the paper's
channel realized along a path rather than compared across steady states.
Stationarizing by the nominal-growth trend $(1+\mu)^t$ makes the system
autonomous; the terminal condition pins $\hat{P}_T$ at the green
steady-state price level, and the government budget holds by construction
at every trial path ($\tau_t = r^{ss}\, b_t + g_{g,t}$). The solver is an
Anderson-accelerated fixed point on the log price path: the underlying map
moves each $\hat P_t$ toward $B_t/S_t$ (excess asset demand at $t$
\emph{lowers} $\hat P_t$, raising the real value $B_t/P_t$ of the fixed
nominal stock until it meets demand --- the transition form of
$P^{*}=B/S$), and because a change in $\hat P_t$ moves the realized real
return at dates $t$ and $t+1$ with opposite signs --- a cross-date
coupling under which plain diagonal relaxation stalls --- the update
combines the most recent residuals by least squares (type-II Anderson,
memory five), with a per-iteration trust region and the market-clearing
residuals reported at every date. Convergence is assessed on the
\emph{free} unknowns $\hat P_1,\dots,\hat P_{T-1}$; the pinned terminal
date's residual is reported separately as a horizon-adequacy check --- it
vanishes only when $T$ is long enough for the wealth distribution to
reach the green steady state, at which point green-steady-state savings
equal $B_T/P_T$ by construction. A path that is not both converged and
horizon-adequate is returned as such and cannot be presented as a result.
Each iteration costs $T$ exact Bellman steps plus $T$
distribution pushes on the full $(a,e)$ grids --- deliberately the
computationally heaviest object in the package. Two experiments run by
default: the \emph{nominal appropriation} (the anchor-exposed design) and
the \emph{indexed mandate} (the anchor-insulated design of
Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}); the comparison of their announcement
jumps is the dynamic version of the anchor-insulation result, and the
model's central untested prediction --- that a credible green
announcement produces \emph{disinflation on impact} under deficit
finance --- becomes a computed, sign-testable object.

The converged run ($n_a = 500$, $T = 80$ years, medium damages, $\beta$
calibrated on this same grid so that pre-announcement real debt matches
the $1.10$ target; interior market-clearing residual below $5\times10^{-4}$
at every free date, terminal residual below $5\times10^{-4}$, $88$ and
$72$ Anderson iterations for the two designs) delivers the paper's
dynamics in three statements. \emph{First, green disinflation is
front-loaded:} the announcement moves the stationarized price level down
$3.9\%$ on impact --- year-one inflation of $-2.0\%$ against the $+2\%$
nominal trend --- which is $77\%$ of the entire long-run green
disinflation ($-5.1\%$ across steady states) realized in the announcement
year, before the first unit of green capital is installed. The price
level jumps because asset demand does: households capitalize the future
damage reduction and the transition-long tax path immediately, and the
price level is what clears that market. Inflation is back within a
quarter-point of trend by year two --- and stays there --- while green
capital accumulates over decades: the announcement effect and the
accumulation dynamics separate cleanly.
\emph{Second, the revaluation channel is a one-time announcement windfall:}
the surprise repricing hands holders of the outstanding nominal stock a
real gain of $5.3\%$ of the program's present value ($5.3\%$ under the
indexed mandate) --- the dynamic realization of the negative
$\nu_{\text{reval}}$ of the steady-state decomposition, collected entirely
in the announcement year. \emph{Third, the denomination of the green
budget is irrelevant on the equilibrium path:} the nominal-appropriation
and indexed-mandate designs produce announcement jumps within two
hundredths of a percentage point of each other and land on the same
terminal steady state, so the anchor-insulation value of the indexed
mandate (Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}) is entirely
\emph{off}-equilibrium --- it removes the self-fulfilling brown-stagnation
equilibria without changing the equilibrium the economy actually follows.
The contrast with the Phillips-curve exercises is the point of this
computation: the linearized heterogeneous-agent exercise prices a
comparable deficit-financed green announcement (a quasi-permanent program of
one percent of output) at $+0.4\%$ impact inflation (the demand stimulus),
while the demand-theory computation prices its permanent program at
$-2.0\%$ (the asset-demand shift) --- opposite signs for the same class of
announcement, because the
two exercises answer with different mechanisms, and only the latter
contains the price-level-as-market-clearing margin the paper is about. This
transition is the converged, horizon-adequate, grid-consistent run
described in Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}.

\pfigguard{PFig18_dtpl_transition.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig18_dtpl_transition.pdf}
\caption{The nonlinear price-level transition (converged, grid-consistent
run, $n_a=500$, $T=80$ years, $\beta$ calibrated on this grid to the
$1.10$ debt target): stationarized price level, inflation, real debt
versus asset demand, green capital, damages, and market-clearing
residuals, for the nominal-appropriation and indexed-mandate designs. The
announcement jump (the $-3.9\%$ price-level drop between the
pre-announcement level and year one) front-loads $77\%$ of the long-run
green disinflation; inflation is within a quarter-point of the $2\%$
trend by year two; the two designs are nearly indistinguishable on the
equilibrium path. Residuals clear to $5\times10^{-4}$ at every date.}
\label{fig:pfig18}
\end{figure}}

% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}
% ==========================================================================

In an economy in which the price level is what clears the market for
precautionary savings, the fiscal arithmetic of the green transition is
neither the FTPL's nor the New Keynesian one. A permanent green investment
program financed by nominal debt pays for a large fraction of itself, but
through the damage dividend rather than the inflation tax: productive green
deficits are \emph{disinflationary}, and their revaluation channel hands
bondholders a windfall --- a distributional fact that should reshape how the
politics of green finance are modeled. The denomination of the green budget
is a determinacy instrument: nominal appropriations expose the economy to
self-fulfilling brown stagnation when damage incidence is regressive enough,
while indexed mandates insulate the nominal anchor at no fiscal cost. The
financing mix is a price-level instrument: replacing lump-sum deficit
financing with a proportional levy plus rebate flips the revaluation
channel's sign, raises the self-financing share, and converts a regressive
program into a progressive one at identical aggregate cost. And a moderate
steady-state inflation rate is part of the optimal financing mix.
The representative-agent and linearized heterogeneous-agent diagnostics of
Section~\ref{sec:transitions}
indicate that the announced program does not destabilize the nominal block
on the way to the new steady state, and the nonlinear heterogeneous-agent
transition in which the price-level path is itself pinned by asset demand
--- computed in Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} as a converged fixed point with
no Phillips curve --- shows that the mechanism's dynamics are front-loaded:
three quarters of the green disinflation, and the entire bondholder
windfall, arrive in the announcement year. A two-state aggregate climate
process (Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}) makes the nominal safe asset
literally risky and shows that green investment compresses that
systematic risk; the compression proves fiscally inert but valuable as
insurance, most so in the disaster state. Adding production with brown
capital and a carbon price \citep{barrage2020}, and a fully stochastic
transition, are the main open modeling items. The sharpest untested
implication is empirical: a credible, deficit-financed green-investment
announcement should \emph{lower} market-implied expected inflation, against
the New Keynesian sign --- a prediction the confounded reduced-form record
cannot yet adjudicate and that awaits high-frequency identification around
green-fiscal surprises.

\appendix
% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Proofs}\label{app:proofs}

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:S}}
Feasibility: at $(a,e)=(-\bar a, e_{\min})$ maximal consumption is
$y(e_{\min};D)-\tau-r\bar a>0$ on the stated set, so the constraint set
is nonempty and compact at every state; with $\beta<1$ and $u$ bounded above
on compacts (after the usual truncation of the asset grid at
$\bar A<\infty$, valid since $\beta(1+r)<1$ implies a finite buffer-stock
target), the Bellman operator is a contraction and $V$, the policies, and the
invariant distribution exist; continuity in $(\tau,D)$ follows from the
theorem of the maximum and the continuity of the (finite-state) invariant
distribution in the policy. Scaling: let $\lambda=1-D>0$ and scale
$(a,\tau)\mapsto(\lambda a,\lambda\tilde\tau)$ with
$\tilde\tau=\tau/(1-D)$. The budget set scales linearly; with CRRA,
$u(\lambda c)=\lambda^{1-\sigma}u(c)+\text{const}$, so
$V_\lambda(\lambda a,e)=\lambda^{1-\sigma}V_1(a,e)+\text{const}$ and the
optimal policy satisfies $a'_\lambda(\lambda a,e)=\lambda a'_1(a,e)$. The
invariant distribution over rescaled assets is the pushforward of the
original, hence aggregate assets scale by $\lambda$, which is
\eqref{eq:scaling}. With $\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$ the endowment process itself
depends on $D$ and the argument gives only the decomposition into level and
risk components. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:climate}}
Write the right side of \eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} as $T(D)$ on
$[0,D_{\max}]$. $T$ is continuous, $T(0)>0$, $T(D_{\max})<D_{\max}$, and
$T'(D) = -D_{\max}e^{-\gamma_x X(D)}\gamma_x \varepsilon_0
(1-\alpha_A A)/\delta_x \cdot(-1) \cdot(-1) <0$... computing directly:
$X(D)=\varepsilon_0(1-\alpha_A A)(1-D)/\delta_x$ is decreasing in $D$, and
$D\mapsto D_{\max}(1-e^{-\gamma_x X})$ is increasing in $X$; hence $T$ is
\emph{decreasing} in $D$. A continuous decreasing self-map of an interval has
exactly one fixed point. Comparative statics: $g_g\uparrow \Rightarrow
K_g\uparrow \Rightarrow A\uparrow \Rightarrow X(\cdot)\downarrow \Rightarrow
T(\cdot)\downarrow$ pointwise, so the fixed point falls; strict when
$\theta_g\alpha_A>0$. Under the nominal regime $g_g=G_g/P$ is strictly
decreasing in $P$, delivering the stated monotonicity. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:anchor}}
Constant real debt $b=B_t/P_t$ across stationary periods forces
$P_{t+1}/P_t=B_{t+1}/B_t=1+\mu$; the Fisher equation then gives
$1+r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:determinacy}}
(i) $\Phi$ is continuous on the feasibility set (Lemma~\ref{lem:S};
$D$ constant here). Low-$P$ boundary: as $P$ approaches the feasibility edge
from above, $\tau(P)=(r^{ss}B+G_g)/P$ rises toward the bound while
$B/P\to$ its maximal value on the set; $\Sfun$ remains bounded (buffer-stock
target finite under $\beta(1+r)<1$), so $\Phi<0$ there for $B$ large enough
relative to the bounded demand --- and in any case for $P$ small enough that
$B/P$ exceeds the demand bound. High-$P$ boundary: $\tau(P)\to0$,
$\Sfun\to\Sfun(1+r^{ss};0,D)>0$, $B/P\to0$, so $\Phi>0$. The intermediate
value theorem yields a zero. (ii) Where $\varepsilon_S>-1$,
$d\Phi/dP=(\Sfun/P)(\varepsilon_S+1)>0$ at any zero: all crossings are upward,
and a continuous function with only upward crossings has exactly one.
(iii) $(K_g,X,D)$ solve \eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} given
$g_g$; with $\theta_g=0$ or the mandate, $D$ does not vary with $P$, so the
block is recursive and does not enter $\Phi$'s dependence on $P$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:selffinancing} and
Result \ref{res:disinflation}}
(i) is Definition~\ref{def:nu} plus the budget identity. (ii) The net burden
is $N=\tau+D$ with $\tau=r^{ss}B/P+g_g$. Under the nominal budget $g_g=G_g/P$,
so $dg_g = dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - g_g\,d\ln P$ with
$dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}=dG_g/P$; and $dD=-\kappa\,dg_g$. Differentiating
$\tau$ gives $d\tau = -(r^{ss}B/P)\,d\ln P + dg_g$, so
\[
dN = d\tau + dD = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g - (r^{ss}B/P)\,d\ln P.
\]
Substituting $dg_g = dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - g_g\,d\ln P$ and using
$r^{ss}B/P = \tau - g_g$,
\[
dN = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}
   - \big[(r^{ss}B/P)+(1-\kappa)g_g\big]\,d\ln P
   = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - (\tau-\kappa g_g)\,d\ln P,
\]
which is \eqref{eq:marginal} after writing
$d\ln P = \epsilon_P\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}$ (around the no-program state
$g_g=0$, where $dg_g=dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}$).
(iii) is the incidence statement plus the Gini comparison computed at the
invariant distribution. Result~\ref{res:disinflation}: $P=B/\Sfun$ at both
steady states, so $P_1<P_0 \iff \Sfun_1>\Sfun_0$;
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}\propto(1/P_0-1/P_1)=(\Sfun_0-\Sfun_1)/B$. The stated
sufficient conditions each raise $\Sfun_1$ relative to $\Sfun_0$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:sunspots}}
On the interval where $\varepsilon_S<-1$, every crossing of $\Phi$ is
downward ($d\Phi/dP<0$); the boundary conditions give $\Phi<0$ at the low
edge and $\Phi>0$ at the high edge. A continuous function that starts
negative, ends positive, and possesses a downward crossing must cross zero at
least twice more around it (sign-change counting): at least two zeros in
total, generically three with the middle one unstable under the natural
tatonnement. Pareto ranking with $\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$: at $P_L^{*}$ both
the level and the risk components of effective income improve for every
household with $\chi(e)>0$; utilitarian $W$ is higher (and is evaluated
exactly at each root by the package). Self-fulfillment is
Definition~\ref{def:equilibrium}: each root satisfies every equilibrium
condition. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:insulation} and corollaries}
Under the mandate $dD/dP=0$, so
$\varepsilon_S=\eta_\tau\,d\ln\tau/d\ln P$ and
$d\ln\tau/d\ln P=-(r^{ss}B/P)/\tau\in(-1,0]$ since $\tau=r^{ss}B/P+g_g\ge
r^{ss}B/P$ (for $g_g\ge0$; when $r^{ss}<0$ the ratio is positive and small,
and the bound holds a fortiori with $|d\ln\tau/d\ln P|=|r^{ss}|b/\tau<1$
whenever $\tau>|r^{ss}|b$, the empirically relevant region --- the package
checks it). Hence $|\varepsilon_S|\le|\eta_\tau|<1$ under the verified
magnitude, and Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}(ii) applies for every
$\theta_g,\psi,\phi_D$. Corollary~\ref{cor:anchor} combines this with
Lemma~\ref{lem:anchor}; Corollary~\ref{cor:reversal} contrasts with the
nominal-rule uniqueness of \citet{hagedorn2026dtpl};
Corollary~\ref{cor:sargentwallace} is the classical argument: with no nominal
object in the equilibrium conditions, $P$ and $2P$ support identical real
allocations. \qed

\subsection{Proof sketch of Proposition \ref{prop:financingdesign}}
Consider the budget-neutral perturbation $d\tau_{ls} = -(1-D)\,d\vartheta <
0$ at fixed $g_g$ and $D$. The demand response is
$d\Sfun = \Sfun_{\tau}\, d\tau_{ls} + \Sfun_{\vartheta}\, d\vartheta$. By
condition (i), $\Sfun_{\tau} \ge 0$, so the first term is negative. For the
second, write the levy as the proportional scaling of effective endowments
by $(1-\vartheta)$; by the scaling logic of Lemma~\ref{lem:S}, a pure
proportional contraction scales asset demand approximately one-for-one
(exactly, when the lump-sum component is rescaled alongside; the residual
non-homotheticity is second-order for small $\vartheta$), so
$\Sfun_{\vartheta} \approx -\Sfun/(1-\vartheta) < 0$. Both terms lower
$\Sfun$; hence $P = B/\Sfun$ rises and
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} = r^{ss}B\,(1/P_0 - 1/P_1)/g_{g,1}$ rises. The rebate
design extends the same perturbation past $\tau_{ls} = r^{ss}b$ into
negative lump-sum territory ($\tau_{ls} < r^{ss}b$), and with
$\Sfun_{\tau} \ge 0$ each further unit of rebate financed by the levy
continues to lower $\Sfun$. The ordering
$P^{*}_{\text{deficit}} < P^{*}_{\text{mixed}} < P^{*}_{\text{levy}} <
P^{*}_{\text{rebate}}$ and the associated $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ ranking are
verified exactly in Table~\ref{tab:regimes}. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:optimalmu}}
By the envelope theorem on the household problem, the welfare effect of a
small change $d\tau<0$ funded by the revaluation is
$-\int u'(c)\,d\tau\,\dd\Omega>0$ weighted by population marginal utilities,
while the bondholders' capital loss (or forgone windfall) is weighted by
$\int u'(c)\,a\,\dd\Omega / \int a\,\dd\Omega$, the asset-weighted marginal
utility, which is strictly smaller when wealth and consumption are positively
associated and wealth is concentrated. The climate cost of $d\mu$ operates
through $dg_g=-g_g\,d\ln P<0$ and is proportional to the program size
$g_g$; for small programs it is dominated at $\mu=0^{+}$. Interior optimum
then follows from continuity of $W(\mu)$ on the existence region and
deterioration at large $\mu$ (buffer-return and climate erosion), both
verified on the grid. \qed

\subsection{Fiscal inertness of the climate-risk premium}
The ergodic-expected real interest bill in the aggregate-risk economy is
independent of the risk premium. With realized gross real return
$R(s,s')=(1+r^{ss})P_s/P_{s'}$ and state-contingent real debt $b_s=B/P_s$,
\begin{align*}
\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'}\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\big(R(s,s')-1\big)b_s
&=\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'}\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}
   \Big[(1+r^{ss})\tfrac{B}{P_{s'}}-\tfrac{B}{P_s}\Big]\\
&=(1+r^{ss})B\sum_{s'}\Big(\textstyle\sum_s \pi_s \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\Big)\tfrac{1}{P_{s'}}
  - B\sum_s \pi_s \tfrac{1}{P_s}\\
&=(1+r^{ss})B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big]-B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big]
  = r^{ss}B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big],
\end{align*}
where the third line uses the ergodicity of $\pi$
($\sum_s \pi_s \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}=\pi_{s'}$). The premium
$\mathbb{E}[R]-r^{ss}>0$ therefore adds nothing to the government's expected
budget: it is a risk-timing wedge that redistributes the debt-service cost
across states but leaves its ergodic mean at $r^{ss}$ times the debt. Its
value is a household risk-sharing object, priced in
Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}. \qed

% ==========================================================================
\section{The New Keynesian model used in the transition
diagnostics}\label{app:nkmodel}
% ==========================================================================

The economy of Section~\ref{sec:model} is an \emph{endowment} economy:
households receive the effective endowment $y(e;D)$ of
\eqref{eq:incidence}, and the price level is determined by asset-market
clearing of the nominal debt stock, $P^\ast=B/\Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau,D)$. The
two Phillips-curve exercises of Sections~\ref{subsec:ranktrans} and
\ref{subsec:hanktier1} embed the \emph{same} household preferences
\eqref{eq:preferences}, climate sector
\eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint}, and government
\eqref{eq:nominalrule}--\eqref{eq:gbc} in a New Keynesian production
economy: output is produced from labor, monopolistic firms face sticky
prices, and the central bank sets the nominal rate by an interest-rate
rule. Inflation is then determined by a New Keynesian Phillips curve and
the policy rule rather than by asset-market clearing --- which is exactly
why these exercises are diagnostics of transition \emph{shape} and cannot,
by construction, represent the demand-theory price-level mechanism. This
appendix states the environment. Both versions share the production,
pricing, policy, fiscal, and climate blocks (Appendix~\ref{app:nkcommon})
and differ only in the household side: a representative consumer
(Appendix~\ref{app:nkrank}) or the incomplete-markets household of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} augmented with a labor choice
(Appendix~\ref{app:nkhank}). Time is quarterly here; stationarized
real quantities and net inflation $\pi_t$ are dated so that $x_{t-1}$
denotes a predetermined stock.

\subsection{Common blocks}\label{app:nkcommon}

\paragraph{Production and marginal cost.} A unit continuum of
monopolistically competitive firms produces differentiated goods from labor
with economy-wide total factor productivity $Z$ (normalized $Z=1$) net of
climate damages $D$. Aggregate output and real marginal cost are
\begin{equation}
Y_t = (1-D_t)\,Z\,N_t, \qquad
mc_t = \frac{w_t}{(1-D_t)\,Z},
\label{eq:nk-production}
\end{equation}
where $N_t$ is aggregate effective labor and $w_t$ the real wage. Damages
enter as a TFP tax, the production-economy counterpart of the endowment
haircut $y(e;D)$ in Section~\ref{sec:model}.

\paragraph{Sticky prices.} Firms face quadratic price-adjustment costs
(Rotemberg) with steady-state gross markup
$\mu_p=\varepsilon_p/(\varepsilon_p-1)$ --- written $\mu_p$ to keep it
distinct from the nominal-growth rate $\mu$ of Section~\ref{sec:model} ---
and a slope parameter governing price stickiness. This yields the New
Keynesian Phillips curve, written in the exact nonlinear (Rotemberg) form
in the representative-agent version and in the equivalent log form used by
the sequence-space solver in the heterogeneous-agent version (see the two
subsections below).

\paragraph{Monetary policy and the Fisher equation.} The central bank sets
the net nominal rate $i_t$ by an interest-rate rule, and the ex-post real
return on the one-period nominal bond obeys the Fisher relation
\begin{equation}
1 + r_t = \frac{1+i_{t-1}}{1+\pi_t},
\label{eq:nk-fisher}
\end{equation}
the dynamic counterpart of the steady-state Fisher equation
\eqref{eq:fisher}: a surprise increase in $\pi_t$ lowers the realized real
return on predetermined nominal positions, which is the linearized
redistribution channel of the paper.

\paragraph{Fiscal policy.} Real government debt $b_t=B_t/P_t$ evolves as
\begin{equation}
b_t = (1+r_t)\,b_{t-1} + g_{g,t} - \tau_t, \qquad
\tau_t = r^{ss}B + \phi_b\,(b_{t-1}-B),
\label{eq:nk-fiscal}
\end{equation}
so that the debt-stabilization speed $\phi_b$ indexes how fast taxes retire
program-induced debt: a low $\phi_b$ deficit-finances the program on impact
(the benchmark), a high $\phi_b$ approximates contemporaneous tax finance.
Real green investment follows a quasi-permanent autoregressive process,
\begin{equation}
g_{g,t} = \rho_g\, g_{g,t-1} + \varepsilon_{g,t},
\label{eq:nk-program}
\end{equation}
with persistence $\rho_g$ close to one (the announced program is nearly
permanent).

\paragraph{Climate.} The climate block is \emph{identical} to
Section~\ref{subsec:climate}: abatement capital, the carbon stock, and
damages evolve as
\begin{align}
K_{g,t} &= (1-\delta_g)\,K_{g,t-1} + g_{g,t}, \label{eq:nk-kg}\\
X_t &= (1-\delta_x)\,X_{t-1}
      + \varepsilon_0\bigl(1-\alpha_A(1-e^{-\theta_g K_{g,t-1}})\bigr)Y_t,
      \label{eq:nk-carbon}\\
D_t &= D_{\max}\bigl(1-e^{-\gamma_x X_{t-1}}\bigr),
      \label{eq:nk-damages}
\end{align}
with the parameters of Section~\ref{sec:quant}.

\subsection{Representative-agent version}\label{app:nkrank}

A representative household with CRRA utility (risk aversion $\sigma$, the
same coefficient as in Section~\ref{subsec:households}) and Frisch elasticity
$\nu$ chooses consumption $c_t$, labor $n_t$, and one-period nominal bonds.
The private optimum, technology, Rotemberg Phillips curve, resource
constraint, and policy rule are
\begin{align}
c_t^{-\sigma} &= \beta\,\frac{1+i_t}{1+\pi_{t+1}}\,c_{t+1}^{-\sigma},
  &&\text{(Euler)} \label{eq:rank-euler}\\
n_t^{1/\nu} &= w_t\,c_t^{-\sigma}, &&\text{(labor supply)} \label{eq:rank-labor}\\
\pi_t(1+\pi_t) &= \beta\Bigl(\tfrac{c_{t+1}}{c_t}\Bigr)^{-\sigma}
  \pi_{t+1}(1+\pi_{t+1})\,\tfrac{Y_{t+1}}{Y_t}
  + \tfrac{\varepsilon_p}{\kappa_r}\Bigl(mc_t-\tfrac{\varepsilon_p-1}{\varepsilon_p}\Bigr),
  &&\text{(NKPC)} \label{eq:rank-nkpc}\\
Y_t &= c_t + g_{g,t} + \tfrac{\kappa_r}{2}\pi_t^2\,Y_t,
  &&\text{(resource)} \label{eq:rank-resource}\\
i_t &= \rho_i\, i_{t-1} + (1-\rho_i)\Bigl[(\tfrac{1}{\beta}-1)
  + \phi_\pi\,\pi_t - \psi_g\bigl(K_g^{ss}-K_{g,t}\bigr)\Bigr],
  &&\text{(policy)} \label{eq:rank-policy}
\end{align}
together with \eqref{eq:nk-production}, \eqref{eq:nk-fiscal}, the program
\eqref{eq:nk-program}, and the climate block
\eqref{eq:nk-kg}--\eqref{eq:nk-damages}. Here $\kappa_r$ is the Rotemberg
adjustment-cost coefficient and $\varepsilon_p$ the elasticity of
substitution across varieties. The policy rule allows interest-rate inertia
($\rho_i$) and a temporary green accommodation tied to the green-capital gap
($\psi_g$), fading as $K_{g,t}\to K_g^{ss}$; the four monetary regimes of
Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans} are parameterizations of
\eqref{eq:rank-policy}. Perfect-foresight paths are computed by a
nonlinear boundary-value solver from the pre-program steady state to the
green terminal steady state.

\subsection{Heterogeneous-agent version}\label{app:nkhank}

The one-asset heterogeneous-agent economy of
Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1} replaces the representative consumer with the
incomplete-markets household of Section~\ref{subsec:households}, adding an
intratemporal labor choice. A household in idiosyncratic state $e$ (the
Markov chain of Section~\ref{subsec:households}) with beginning-of-period
real bond holdings $a_{t-1}$ solves
\begin{align}
c_t^{-\sigma} &= \beta\,(1+r_{t+1})\,c_{t+1}^{-\sigma}
   \quad\perp\quad a_t \ge -\bar a, &&\text{(Euler, borrowing constraint)}
   \label{eq:hank-euler}\\
c_t + a_t &= (1+r_t)\,a_{t-1} + w_t\, n_t\, e + (\mathrm{Div}_t-\tau_t)\,e,
   &&\text{(budget)} \label{eq:hank-budget}\\
\vphi\, n_t^{1/\nu} &= w_t\, e\, c_t^{-\sigma},
   &&\text{(labor supply)} \label{eq:hank-labor}
\end{align}
where $\sigma$ and $\nu$ are the CRRA coefficient and Frisch elasticity as
above, $\vphi$ the labor-disutility weight, $\mathrm{Div}_t$ real dividends
rebated in proportion to efficiency $e$, and $\perp$ denotes the
complementary-slackness form of the borrowing constraint. The household's asset $a$ is the real value of the nominal
government bond, exactly as in \eqref{eq:budget}: surprise inflation
revalues $a_{t-1}$ through $r_t$ in \eqref{eq:nk-fisher}, state by state.
Writing $\mathrm{SUM}(\cdot)$ for the cross-sectional aggregate over the
joint distribution of $(a,e)$, the aggregate block is
\begin{align}
N_t &= \frac{Y_t}{(1-D_t)Z}, &&\text{(labor demand)} \label{eq:hank-labdemand}\\
\mathrm{Div}_t &= Y_t - w_t N_t
   - \frac{\mu_p}{\mu_p-1}\,\frac{\log(1+\pi_t)^2}{2\kappa_p}\,Y_t,
   &&\text{(dividends)} \label{eq:hank-div}\\
\kappa_p\Bigl(\frac{w_t}{(1-D_t)Z}-\frac{1}{\mu_p}\Bigr)
   &+ \frac{Y_{t+1}}{Y_t}\,\frac{\log(1+\pi_{t+1})}{1+r_{t+1}}
   - \log(1+\pi_t) = 0, &&\text{(NKPC)} \label{eq:hank-nkpc}\\
i_t &= r^{ss} + \phi_\pi\,\pi_t - \psi_g\, g_{g,t}, &&\text{(policy)} \label{eq:hank-policy}\\
\mathrm{SUM}(a_t) &= b_t, \qquad \mathrm{SUM}(n_t\, e) = N_t,
   &&\text{(market clearing)} \label{eq:hank-clearing}
\end{align}
together with the Fisher equation \eqref{eq:nk-fisher}, the fiscal block
\eqref{eq:nk-fiscal}, the program \eqref{eq:nk-program}, and the climate
block \eqref{eq:nk-kg}--\eqref{eq:nk-damages}, where $\kappa_p$ is the
(log-form) Phillips-curve slope. The discount factor $\beta$ and the
labor-disutility weight $\vphi$ are calibrated so that the asset market
\eqref{eq:hank-clearing} and the labor market clear at the damaged steady
state with debt at the target of Section~\ref{sec:quant}. The system is
solved by sequence-space Jacobians: the household block delivers the
Jacobians of aggregate consumption, savings, and labor supply with respect
to the paths of $\{r_t,w_t,\tau_t,\mathrm{Div}_t\}$, which are combined with
the aggregate equations \eqref{eq:hank-labdemand}--\eqref{eq:hank-clearing}
to produce the impulse responses of
Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1}. Because \eqref{eq:hank-nkpc} pins inflation
off the Phillips curve, this exercise --- like the representative-agent one
--- does not determine the price \emph{level} by asset demand; that is the
object of the nonlinear transition of Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}, whose
household problem is the same \eqref{eq:hank-euler}--\eqref{eq:hank-budget}
solved globally with the price path, not the Phillips curve, as the unknown.

% ==========================================================================
\section{Numerical method}\label{app:numerics}

The household problem is solved by value function iteration with Howard
acceleration on a 500-node exponentially spaced asset grid (choices on the
grid; no reliance on monotonicity), the invariant distribution by exact power
iteration on the sparse joint transition (no simulation), both inherited from
the underlying replication package and validated against its 10-point check
suite. Aggregate asset demand on the $(\tau,D)$ plane is precomputed on a
$7\times4$ node grid at the policy real rate and interpolated bilinearly;
every reported equilibrium is \emph{re-solved exactly} at the root (the
interpolation residual is reported). Roots of $\Phi$ are found by a dense
scan plus bracketed one-dimensional root-finding, returning \emph{all} roots.
Under the incidence
gradient the high-$(\tau,D)$ corners of the interpolation grid can be
infeasible; the $\tau$-range is clamped at the feasibility bound evaluated at
the median damage node, remaining infeasible corner nodes are filled by
nearest-in-$\tau$ values \emph{for scanning purposes only}, and every reported
equilibrium is re-solved exactly at the root (the interpolation residual is
reported), so filled cells cannot silently produce spurious equilibria. The
extended experiments are evaluated at the accommodative stance
$\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$: at $\mu=0.02$ the incidence-amplified precautionary
demand pushes $P^{*}$ so low that the implied tax burden crosses the lump-sum
feasibility bound and no stationary equilibrium exists --- a fiscal-space
collapse reported as a result in its own right. The elasticity
$\varepsilon_S(P)$ is computed by centered log-differences along the demand
curve. The optimal-policy exercise rebuilds the interpolant at each $\mu$
(the real rate changes). Runtime: about one minute per full pass at
$n_a=500$ on commodity hardware (68 s in the authors' benchmark run); a FAST
mode at $n_a=100$ reproduces all qualitative results.

\section{Supplementary figures}\label{app:figures}
% ==========================================================================
% Supporting figures for results whose main statements are made in the body;
% the body carries the figures central to the narrative and refers here for
% the bounding, robustness, extended-welfare, and two-asset detail.

This appendix collects the supporting figures for four results whose headline
magnitudes are reported in the text: the bounds on the revaluation channel
(Section~\ref{subsec:maturity}), the calibration-robustness frontier
(Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}), the extended welfare incidence
(Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}), the aggregate-risk welfare decomposition
(Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}), and the two-asset heterogeneous-agent
impulse responses (Section~\ref{sec:transitions}).

self-financing share $\nu$ over the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane; the white line
is the $\nu=1$ frontier (full self-financing), and the marked column
values $D_0\in\{0.02,0.06,0.20\}$ are the sourced damage calibrations of
Table~\ref{tab:sources}. The red markers are the benchmark
$\theta_g=1.2$ at the sourced columns. (b) One-at-a-time sensitivity at the
medium column under the held debt-target protocol; the dashed line is full
financing. ($\sigma=1$ has no bar: the debt target is infeasible at that
risk aversion, as the text explains.)}
